title: 1.1: Explains the two-stage matching process in Scapy's sr/sr1 (e.g., hashret() for bucket lookup followed by answers() for validation)
1.2: Identifies a configuration option (e.g., conf.checkIPinIP) that affects packet matching for tunneled traffic, distinct from conf.checkIPsrc and conf.checkIPaddr
1.3: Reports that tunneled packets with different outer destinations (e.g., IP(dst="2.2.2.2")/IP() vs IP(dst="3.3.3.3")/IP()) produce identical hashret values even when inner payloads match
1.4: Explains that hashret() skips outer IP addressing for IP-in-IP packets (e.g., proto 4 or 41) when conf.checkIPinIP is False
1.5: Reports that answers() returns True for packet pairs that should not match (e.g., response from gateway A matching probe sent to gateway B) when checkIPinIP is False
1.6: Explains why tcpdump shows correct packet flow while Scapy mismatches (e.g., tcpdump examines wire bytes vs Scapy uses computed hashes)
1.7: Identifies the role of the hsent dictionary in the matching process (e.g., storing sent packets keyed by hashret for lookup)
1.8: Demonstrates the hashret collision with example (e.g., Python commands creating tunneled packets and comparing their hashret values)
